American Revolutionary War: World's first submarine attack – The American submersible craft Turtle attempts to attach a time bomb to the hull of British Admiral Richard Howe's flagship HMS Eagle in New York Harbor.
A fire destroys major parts of the town of Askersund, Sweden.
Morocco recognizes the United States as a sovereign state.
American Revolutionary War – Battle of Cooch's Bridge: British and Hessian forces defeat an American militia in a minor skirmish in New Castle County, Delaware.
The Stars and Stripes is adopted by the Continental Congress as the flag of the United States.

American Revolutionary War: In Paris, the Treaty of Alliance and the Treaty of Amity and Commerce are signed by the United States and France, signaling official French recognition of the new republic.
King George III appoints the five-member Carlisle Peace Commission to present peace terms to negotiate an end to the rebellion of Britain's 13 American colonies.

The 1,800 feet (550 m) long Hudson River Chain, designed to prevent British ships from moving up the river toward West Point, New York is stretched across the river and anchored by an engineering team under the direction of Captain Thomas Machin.
A joint Spanish-Portuguese survey of the Amazon basin begins to determine the boundary between the colonial possessions in South America; it continues until 1795.

Action of 17 August ____: The 64-gun British warship HMS Ardent is captured by France in the English Channel off of Plymouth after an ineffective attempt by the British captain to properly aim its cannons at the French frigate Junon.
Captain James Cook is killed on the Sandwich Islands, on his third voyage.
Emperor Kōkaku accedes to the throne of Japan.
American Revolutionary War – Battle of Cape St. Vincent: British Admiral Sir George Rodney defeats a Spanish fleet.
American Revolutionary War – Battle of Camden: British troops inflict heavy losses on a Patriot army at Camden, South Carolina.
American Revolutionary War – Battle of Cowpens: The American Continental Army, under Daniel Morgan, decisively defeats British forces in South Carolina.
American Revolution: American and French troops begin a siege of the British at Yorktown, Virginia.
General John Campbell, defender of the British colony of West Florida, surrenders the capital at Pensacola to Spanish forces commanded by Bernardo de Galvez.
Chief Kamehameha I of Hawaii gains control of the northern part of the island of Hawaii, after defeating his cousin Kīwalaʻō.
Gnadenhutten massacre: In Ohio, 29 Native American men, 27 women, and 34 children are killed by colonial militiamen in retaliation for raids carried out by another Native American group.
The British House of Commons votes against further war in America, paving the way for the Second Rockingham ministry and the Peace of Paris.
In Paris, Jean-François Pilâtre de Rozier and François Laurent, marquis d'Arlandes, make the first untethered hot air balloon flight (flight time: 25 minutes, Maximum height: 900 m).
Jacques Charles and Les Frères Robert launch the world's first hydrogen-filled balloon, Le Globe, in Paris.

Sébastien Lenormand makes the first ever recorded public demonstration of a parachute descent, by jumping from the tower of the Montpellier Observatory in France, using his rigid-framed model, which he intends as a form of fire escape.
The Captivity of Mangalorean Catholics at Seringapatam begins.
Henry Cavendish's paper to the Royal Society of London, Experiments on Air, reveals the composition of water.
The Methodist Episcopal Church in the United States is officially formed at the "Christmas Conference", led by Thomas Coke and Francis Asbury.
Coal gas is first used for illumination.
Thomas Jefferson is appointed the new U.S. Minister to France, and Benjamin Franklin's request for permission to return home is accepted.
The Confederation Congress votes an $80,000 expense to establish diplomatic relations with Morocco.
The Confederation Congress establishes the United States Mint to make common coinage and currency for the U.S., to replace individual state coins.
Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart's opera The Marriage of Figaro premieres in Vienna.
The oldest musical organization in the United States, the Stoughton Musical Society, is founded.

Biblical theology becomes a separate discipline from biblical studies, as Johann Philipp Gabler delivers his speech "On the proper distinction between biblical and dogmatic theology and the specific objectives of each" upon his inauguration as the professor of theology at the University of Altdorf in Germany.
Orangist troops attack Vreeswijk, Harmelen and Maarssen; civil war starts in the Dutch Republic.
By a vote of 33 to 29, Harrisburg is approved as the new capital of Pennsylvania.
The Great New Orleans Fire destroys 856 buildings, including St. Louis Cathedral and The Cabildo, leaving most of the town in ruins.

English captains Thomas Gilbert and John Marshall, returning from Botany Bay, become the first Europeans to encounter the Gilbert Islands in the Pacific Ocean. They also chart islands in "Lord Mulgrove's range", later known as the Marshall Islands.
Georgia ratifies the United States Constitution, and becomes the fourth U.S. state under the new government.
Georgetown University is founded in Georgetown, Maryland (part of modern-day Washington, D.C.), as the first Roman Catholic college in the United States.
The Qajar dynasty establish themselves as rulers in Iran.
King Gustav III enforces the Union and Security Act, delivering the coup de grace to Sweden's 70-year-old parliamentarian system, in favor of absolute monarchy.
The first United States Census is authorized; it is held later in the year.
Vincent Ogé leads a rebellion of freed blacks in Saint-Domingue. The rebellion is suppressed and Ogé executed.
Royal assent is given to establishment of the port of Milford Haven in Wales.
George Hammond is appointed as Great Britain's first minister to the United States.
Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart's singspiel opera The Magic Flute (Die Zauberflöte) premières at the Freihaus-Theater auf der Wieden in Vienna with Mozart conducting.
French Revolution: The Papal States lose Avignon to Revolutionary France.
The Insurance Company of North America (later Chubb) is founded in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.
Tipu Sultan invades Kerala, India, but is repulsed.
France's National Convention passes a resolution pledging French support for the overthrow of the governments of other nations.
Pierre Blanchard becomes the first to fly in a gas balloon in the United States.
Oulu Castle in Finland is destroyed in an explosion following the burning of a powder cellar.
The Giles Resolutions are introduced to the United States House of Representatives, asking the House to condemn Alexander Hamilton's handling of loans.
Robespierre establishes the Cult of the Supreme Being as the new state religion of the French First Republic.
Battle of Port-Républicain: British troops capture Port-au-Prince in Haiti from the French.
Battle of Racławice: Polish supporters of the Kościuszko Uprising defeat forces of the Russian Empire.
The Eleventh Amendment to the United States Constitution is passed.
Flanders campaign: Capture of the Dutch fleet at Den Helder: The Dutch fleet, frozen in Zuiderzee, is captured by the French 8th Hussars.
The Third Treaty of Basel is signed, between the French First Republic and the Landgraviate of Hesse-Kassel.
Mungo Park reaches Ségou, the capital of the Bamana Empire.
Annual British iron production reaches 125,000 tons.
The capital of Upper Canada is moved from Newark to York.
Battle of Parramatta: Resistance leader Pemulwuy leads a group of aboriginal warriors, estimated to be at least 100, in an attack on a government farm at Toongabbie in Sydney, Australia.

April 17 Battle of San Juan: Sir Ralph Abercromby unsuccessfully invades San Juan, Puerto Rico in what will be one of the largest British attacks on Spanish territories in the western hemisphere, and one of the worst defeats of the British Royal Navy for years to come.
Siege of Mantua: Field marshal Dagobert von Wurmser surrenders the fortress city to the French; only 16,000 men of the garrison are capable of marching out as prisoners of war.
Battle of Killala: in the last land battle of the Irish Rebellion of ____, British troops defeat the remaining rebel Irish and French forces at Killala.
British whaler John Fearn becomes the first European to land on Nauru.
Edward Jenner publishes An Inquiry into the Causes and Effects of the Variolæ Vaccinæ, describing the smallpox vaccine, in London.
French Revolutionary Wars: At the Battle of Mount Tabor severely outnumbered French forces repulse an Ottoman attack.
War of the Second Coalition: Battle of Wiesloch: Austrian Lieutenant Field Marshal Anton Sztáray defeats the French at Wiesloch.
War of the Second Coalition: Siege of Jaffa – Napoleon captures Jaffa in Palestine from the Ottomans and his troops proceed to kill more than 2,000 Albanian captives.
Fort William College is established by Lord Wellesley, British Governor-General of India, in Calcutta, to promote Bengali, Hindi and other vernaculars of the Indian subcontinent.
The Baker rifle, designed by Ezekiel Baker, is selected by the British Board of Ordnance as a new standard.
The U.S. Library of Congress is founded in Washington, D.C.
An electoral tie between Thomas Jefferson and Aaron Burr is resolved, when Jefferson is elected President of the United States and Burr Vice President by the United States House of Representatives.
Siege of Cairo ends: Cairo falls to British troops.
Giuseppe Piazzi discovers the asteroid and dwarf planet Ceres.
Gia Long captures Hanoi, completing his unification of Vietnam.
Indigenous Australian Pemulwuy, a leader of the resistance to European settlement of Australia, is shot dead by Henry Hacking.
Éleuthère Irénée du Pont founds E. I. du Pont de Nemours and Company, the modern DuPont chemical company, as a gunpowder manufactory near Wilmington, Delaware.
The wagonway between Wandsworth and Croydon is opened, being the first public railway line in England.
Second Anglo-Maratha War: Battle of Assaye – British-led troops defeat Maratha forces.
Kandyan Wars: Kandy, Ceylon is taken by a British detachment.
gumi, predecessor of Shimizu Corporation, a major construction company in Japan, is founded in Kanda region, Edo (modern-day Tokyo).
William Pitt the Younger begins his second term as Prime Minister of the United Kingdom.
Père Lachaise Cemetery a 118-acre (0.48 km) cemetery in Paris, France, is opened.
Napoleonic Wars: War of the Third Coalition – Ulm Campaign: Battle of Elchingen – An Austrian corps under Johann von Riesch is defeated by Marshal Ney, near Elchingen, Bavaria.
The Old Man of the Mountain, a natural rock formation in the U.S. state of New Hampshire, is first mentioned by two workmen, Francis Whitcomb and Luke Brooks.
____ Molise earthquake: 5,573 people in Molise and Campania, Italy, are killed.
January 9 The Dutch commandant of Cape Town surrenders to British forces. On January 10, formal capitulation is signed under the Treaty Tree in Papendorp (modern-day Woodstock).
Battle of Saalfeld: French forces under Marshal Jean Lannes clash at Saalfeld with a Prussian advance guard. The Prussian troops are routed, and Prince Louis Ferdinand of Prussia is killed in combat.
Rana Bahadur Shah is killed by his step-brother Sher Bahadur Shah in the late night meeting which triggers the two weeks' long Bhandarkhal garden massacre in Khatmandu.
Battle of Abrantes, Portugal: The French under Jean-Andoche Junot take the town.
March 25 The United Kingdom Slave Trade Act becomes law abolishing the slave trade in most of the British Empire with effect from 1 May (slavery itself is abolished in British colonies in 1833).
Le Petit Oracle des Dames 42 card deck and booklet are published in Paris by the widow of Gueffier.

January 1 The importation of slaves into the United States is formally banned, as the 1807 Act Prohibiting Importation of Slaves takes effect. However Americans still continue the slave trade by transporting Africans to Cuba and Brazil.

Rum Rebellion: On the 20th anniversary of the foundation of the colony of New South Wales, disgruntled military officers of the New South Wales Corps (the "Rum Corps") overthrow and imprison Governor William Bligh and seize control of the colony.
Battle of Tudela: French Marshal Lannes defeats a Spanish army.
The famous composer Joseph Haydn dies in Vienna at age 77 following Napoleon's second siege of Vienna
British recruits to the British East India Company (and subsequently to the Indian Civil Service) are required to learn at least one Indian language fluently.
King Gustav IV Adolf of Sweden abdicates after the Coup of ____ and is later exiled.
Napoleon decrees that Rome would become the second capital of the French Empire.
Chartered British East Indiaman Elizabeth is wrecked off Dunkirk with the loss of more than 360 lives, many of them lascar seamen.
General Lachlan Macquarie officially becomes Governor of New South Wales.
José Miguel Carrera, Chilean founding father, is sworn in as President of the executive Junta of the government of Chile.
Venezuela declares its Independence from the Spanish Empire.
The Argentine Government declares freedom of expression for the press.
Siege of Cádiz: Marshal Jean-de-Dieu Soult abandons the two-and-a-half-year siege at Cádiz. He withdraws eastward with his forces to join King Joseph Bonaparte and Louis-Gabriel Suchet in Valencia.
Napoleonic Wars – Battle of Vyazma: The rearguard of Napoleon's retreating army is defeated.
New York State charters the City Bank of New York (later Citibank).
Sir Thomas Maitland is appointed as the first Governor of Malta, transforming the island from a British protectorate to a de facto colony.
Napoleonic Wars – Second Battle of Kulm: The Allied Coalition is victorious; Napoleon is forced to halt his advance on Teplitz, and withdraw to Leipzig.
War of 1812 – The second siege of Fort Meigs by British allied forces fails.
War of 1812 – Battle of Valparaíso: Two British Royal Navy ships easily defeat and capture USS Essex and a sister ship off the coast of Chile.

Gaspar de Vigodet, Spain's last colonial administrator of the Viceroyalty of the Río de la Plata (covering nearly all of modern-day Argentina, Chile, Paraguay and Uruguay), surrenders the city of Montevideo to independence fighters, led by General Carlos María de Alvear.

War of 1812 – Burning of Washington: British troops, after defeating American forces at the Battle of Bladensburg, occupy Washington, D.C., setting numerous buildings on fire, including the Capitol and Presidential Mansion.
War of 1812: British troops capture Fort Peter in St. Marys, Georgia, the only battle of the war to take place in the state.
The War of 1812 between the United States and the United Kingdom (including Canada) officially ends, following ratification of the Treaty of Ghent (1814) in Washington, D.C.
8 earthquake shakes northern China causing many houses and caves to collapse, killing at least 13,000 people.
The French frigate Medusa runs aground off the coast of Senegal, with 140 lives lost in the botched rescue that takes weeks, leading to a scandal in the French government.
The Second Bank of the United States obtains its charter.
The Society for the Promotion of Permanent and Universal Peace is founded in London.
The first cholera pandemic originates in Bengal, reaching Calcutta by September.
The frigate HMS Trincomalee is launched in Bombay for the British Royal Navy. She will still be afloat two centuries later.
Sailing through the Sandwich Islands, Otto von Kotzebue discovers New Year Island.
First Seminole War: General Andrew Jackson and his American army invade Florida.

Baji Rao II, Peshwa of the Maratha Confederacy, surrenders to Sir John Malcolm of the British East India Company, bringing an end to the Third Anglo-Maratha War and giving the company control of almost all of modern-day India.
____ United Kingdom general election for the House of Commons. The Tory Party, led by Prime Minister Robert Jenkinson, retains its control of the government but loses some seats.
The new astronomical observatory of Capodimonte in Naples starts operating. The astronomer Carlo Brioschi made the first observation by measuring the position of ⍺ Cassiopeiae.

The Rann of Kutch earthquake, estimated at at least 7.7 Mw kills at least 1,543 people in India in what is now the state of Gujarat at the Arabian Sea, causing an 80–150 km (50–93 mi) stretch of land to be raised as much as 6 m (20 ft) and creating a natural dam, the Allahbund.
Norwich University is founded by Captain Alden Partridge in Vermont as the first private military school in the United States.
The Cato Street Conspiracy, a plot to assassinate Britain's Prime Minister, Lord Liverpool, and his Cabinet is thwarted when police in London arrest 13 plotters after being warned by an informant.
Maine is admitted as the 23rd U.S. state.
Anchor coinage is first struck in silver in London denominated in fractions of the Mauritian dollar for use in British colonies.

The Treaty of Córdoba is signed in Córdoba, Veracruz, Mexico, ratifying the Plan of Iguala of February 24 for Mexico's independence from Spain and, although not recognised by the latter, effectively concludes the Mexican War of Independence.
Widener University is founded in Wilmington, Delaware, as The Bullock School for Boys.
The world's first geographical society, the Société de géographie, is established in Paris.
The Congress of Verona ends having endorsed French Intervention in Spain
The Constituent Congress of Peru begins its first session.
Greek War of Independence: Greek soldiers defeat Ottoman forces at Thermopylae.
May 25 The Duke of Angoulême, commander of the invading French forces, establishes a regency in Madrid on behalf of Ferdinand VII who remains a captive of the Spanish government.
The Basilica of Saint Paul Outside the Walls in Rome is almost completely destroyed by fire.

By secret treaty signed at the Congress of Verona, the Quintuple Alliance gives France a mandate to invade Spain for the purpose of restoring Ferdinand VII (who has been captured by armed revolutionary liberals) as absolute monarch of the country.

Ludwig van Beethoven's Ninth Symphony (the "Choral") premieres at the Theater am Kärntnertor in Vienna. The deaf composer has to be turned around on the stage to witness the enthusiastic audience reaction.
First Anglo-Ashanti War: Battle of Nsamankow – forces of the Ashanti Empire crush British forces in the Gold Coast (modern-day Ghana), killing the British governor Sir Charles MacCarthy.
Charles X succeeds his brother Louis XVIII as King of France.
Thy, once part of the Jutland peninsula forming westernmost Denmark, becomes an island after a flood drowns its 1 kilometre (0.62 mi) wide isthmus.
The Miramichi Fire, a forest fire, breaks out in New Brunswick (Canada).
The U.S. Senate ratifies treaties with the Cheyenne tribe.
Russian mathematician Nikolai Ivanovich Lobachevsky develops non-Euclidean geometry (independently of Janos Bolyai).
The Monkland and Kirkintilloch Railway opens in Scotland.
In France, Nicéphore Niépce makes the first photograph, View from the Window at Le Gras.

The Royal Netherlands Navy's British-built paddle steamer Curaçao sets off on the first transatlantic crossing by steam, departing from Hellevoetsluis in the Netherlands to Paramaribo in the Netherlands South American colony of Dutch Guiana. The ship arrives after a voyage of four weeks.

Voting is completed in elections for France's 430 member Chamber of Deputies. The Ultraroyalistes, supporters of King Charles X, lose their 233-seat majority and finish with 180 seats, the same number as the opposition Doctrinaires.
Battle of Monte Santiago: A squadron of the Brazilian Imperial Navy defeats Argentine vessels after a two-day naval battle.
The Cornwall Bank, earliest constituent of ANZ bank in Australasia, is formed in Launceston, Van Diemens Land.
The Boston Society for Medical Improvement is established in the United States.
Publication (begun on January 14) of Franz Schubert's song cycle Winterreise is concluded posthumously.
Finsteraarhorn, the highest summit of the Bernese Alps, is first ascended.
The patent for an instrument called the accordion is applied for by Cyrill Demian (officially approved on May 23).
Joseph Smith claims to have received the Aaronic Priesthood from John the Baptist.
The Glyptothek museum of classical sculpture, commissioned by King Ludwig I of Bavaria, is opened as Munich's first public museum.
On what becomes called "the Ustertag", men of the Canton of Zürich in Switzerland gather to demand a new constitution.

Belgian Revolution: The army of the United Kingdom of the Netherlands fails to retake Brussels; a National Congress is summoned to draw up a Constitution and a Provisional Government of Belgium is established under Charles Latour Rogier.
Ioannis Kapodistrias, Greek head of state and founder of Greek independence, is assassinated in Nafplion.
Founding of educational establishments: Denison University in Granville, Ohio
First Canut Revolt: After a bloody battle with the military causing 600 deaths, rebellious silk workers seize Lyon, France.

Nullification Crisis: The U.S. state of South Carolina passes the Ordinance of Nullification, challenging the power of the U.S. federal government, by declaring that it will not enforce national tariffs signed into law in 1828 and ____.
U.S. President Andrew Jackson responds to the Nullification Crisis by threatening to send the U.S. Army and Navy into South Carolina if it does not comply.
The City of Buffalo in New York is incorporated.
Foundation of: Kalamazoo College in Kalamazoo, Michigan.
A mob in Jackson County, Missouri, destroys the printing office of the early Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints containing what becomes known as The Doctrine and Covenants.
H.R.H. Prince Mongkut of Siam founds the Dhammayut Buddhist reform movement.
Construction work begins on the Wilberforce Monument in Kingston upon Hull, England.

Imam Faisal bin Turki enters Riyadh and upon entering his father's palace, assassinates his father's murderer, Ibrahim Hamza, and his master; Mishari, and becomes the ruler and founder of the Second Saudi State.

William Lamb, 2nd Viscount Melbourne becomes the last Prime Minister of the United Kingdom to be dismissed by the British monarch. King William IV temporarily appoints Arthur Wellesley, 1st Duke of Wellington, to form a caretaker government.
Louis Havas creates Havas, the first news agency in the world (which later spawns Agence France-Presse).
The Treaty of New Echota is signed between the United States Government and representatives of the Cherokee Nation.
The first Egyptian Museum in Cairo opened.
March 27 Texas Revolution – Goliad massacre: 342 Texan prisoners are shot and killed, along with Texan General James Walker Fannin, by Mexican troops in Goliad, near the Presidio La Bahía.
The New Board brokerage group is founded in New York City.
Chatsworth Head is found near Tamassos, Cyprus.
The Olney Friends School is founded in the Appalachian Mountains of the United States.
The destructive Galilee earthquake causes thousands of deaths in Ottoman Syria.
Charles Dickens's Oliver Twist begins publication in serial form in London.
Isambard Kingdom Brunel's paddle steamer SS Great Western (____) makes the transatlantic crossing to New York from Avonmouth, England, in 15 days, inaugurating a regular steamship service.
Battle of Blood River: The Boers win a decisive victory over the Zulus.
Weenen massacre: Zulu impis massacre about 532 Voortrekkers, Khoikhoi and Basuto around the site of Weenen in South Africa.
The British Aden Expedition captures Aden.
Valley Falls Company, a predecessor of Berkshire Hathaway, a conglomerate and holdings company in the United States, is founded in Rhode Island.
Archaeological excavation at the Mayan site of Copán begins.
The Province of Canada is created by the Act of Union, but not proclaimed until 10 February 1841.

Captain Charles Wilkes' United States Exploring Expedition sights what becomes known as Wilkes Land in the southeast quadrant of Antarctica, claiming it for the United States, and providing evidence that Antarctica is a complete continent.
The Austrian Empire, the United Kingdom, the Kingdom of Prussia and the Russian Empire sign the Convention of London with the Sublime Porte, ruler of the Ottoman Empire.
United States v. The Amistad: The Supreme Court of the United States rules in the case, that the Africans who seized control of the ship had been taken into slavery illegally.
The Georgian province of Guria revolts against the Russian Empire.
The United Kingdom Census is held, the first to record names and approximate ages of every household member, and to be administered nationally.
Giuseppe Verdi's third opera Nabucco premieres at La Scala in Milan; its success establishes Verdi as one of Italy's foremost operatic composers.
Gaylad, ridden by Tom Olliver, wins the Grand National at Aintree Racecourse in England.
Wesleyan University is established in Ohio.
In Barbados, Samuel Jackman Prescod is the first non-white person elected to the House of Assembly.
Eta Carinae flares, to become the second-brightest star.
Saint Louis University School of Law becomes the first law school west of the Mississippi River.
Robert Chambers' anonymous Vestiges of the Natural History of Creation, which paves the way for acceptance of Charles Darwin's The Origin of Species, is published in Britain.
The Columbus and Xenia Railroad, the first railroad planned to be built in Ohio, is chartered.
The first ever international cricket match is played in New York City, United States v Canadian Provinces.
The United States Congress approves the annexation of Texas.
Manifest destiny: U.S. President James K. Polk announces to Congress that the Monroe Doctrine should be strictly enforced, and that the United States should aggressively expand into the West.
A tornado kills 75 in France at Montville while destroying two large factories.
Our Lady of La Salette, a Marian apparition, is said to have been seen by two children at La Salette-Fallavaux in France.
Electric Telegraph Company founded in Britain.
The portion of the District of Columbia in the United States that was ceded by Virginia in 1790 is re-ceded to Virginia.
St. Anthony Hall fraternity is founded at Columbia University, New York City.
After 17 months of travel, Brigham Young leads 148 Mormon pioneers into Salt Lake Valley, resulting in the establishment of Salt Lake City in what becomes Utah.
Faustin Soulouque is elected President of Haiti.

General Anton Puchner, commander of the Austrian armies of Transylvania, declares insurrection against Hungary, and, together with Karl von Urban in the north and the Romanian insurgents led by Avram Iancu, attacks and chases away the Hungarian armed forces occupying Transylvania.
John C. Frémont is court-martialed on grounds of mutiny and disobeying orders. The verdict is set aside by U.S. President James K. Polk, but Frémont retires to California Territory.

The Austrian commander Karl von Urban makes the first stand against the Revolution in Hungary, assembling in his headquarters in Năsăud hundreds of delegates from all districts of the Principality of Transylvania. As a result, 918 communities in the region distance themselves from the Revolution.

April 12 Hungarian Revolution of 1848: Because of his series of defeats suffered from the Hungarian army, Alfred I, Prince of Windisch-Grätz is released from the supreme command of the Austrian forces in Hungary, and replaced by Ludwig von Welden.
The North Carolina General Assembly incorporates the North Carolina Railroad, to complete a rail line from Goldsboro through Raleigh, and Salisbury to Charlotte.
June 5 Denmark becomes a constitutional monarchy.
The first portion of the Oudh Bequest is transferred from Oudh State in the British Raj to the Shia Islam holy cities of Najaf and Karbala, in Persia.
Bingley Hall, the world's first purpose-built exhibition hall, opens in Birmingham, England.
The New Mexico Territory is organized by order of the United States Congress.

Battle of Ras Tanoura, a collision occurs between the Bahrain blockading force off Kateef and the Arab fugitives at Kenn Island, who surprised and attacked the Bahrain fleet. Mubarak bin Abdulla bin Ahmed, along with his brother Rashid and Busher bin Rahma Al Jalahma were all killed in the battle.
Victor Hugo uses the phrase United States of Europe, in a speech to the French National Assembly.
The first YMCA in the United States opens in Boston, Massachusetts.
The first British public toilet for women opens in Bedford Street, London.
Taiping Rebellion: The Taiping army takes Hanyang and begins the siege of Wuchang.
The Devil's Island penal colony opens in the colony of French Guiana.
Indian Railways: The first passenger railway in India opens from Bombay to Thana, Maharashtra, 22 miles (35 km).
Taiping Rebellion: French minister de Bourboulon arrives at the Heavenly Capital, aboard the Cassini.
U.S. Commodore Matthew C. Perry arrives in Edo Bay, Japan, with a request for a trade treaty.
Hard Times begins serialisation in Charles Dickens' magazine, Household Words.
Bryant & Stratton College is founded as a business institute in the United States.
German psychologist Friedrich Eduard Beneke disappears; 2 years later his remains are found in the canal near Charlottenburg.
Emperor of the French Napoleon III, with Empress Eugénie, begins a 6-day state visit to Britain.

In Australia, the province of Victoria (named for then reigning British monarch, Queen Victoria) to the southeast is separated administratively from the earlier larger province of New South Wales to the north.

Queen Victoria of the United Kingdom, with Prince Albert, begins a 10-day state visit to Paris, the first visit of a reigning British monarch to France since 1413. While there, she visits the Exposition Universelle.
Legal protection of widow remarriage is extended in India.
Battle of Osawatomie: Proslavery forces defeat antislavery forces in Bleeding Kansas.
David Livingstone arrives at Quelimane on the Indian Ocean, having completed a 2-year transcontinental journey across Africa from Luanda.
Suzumoto Vaudeville Theater officially opens in Ueno region, Edo (modern-day Tokyo) in Japan.
Elisha Otis' first elevator is installed (at 488 Broadway, New York City).
The Indus Valley Region (i.e., Pakistan Region) is incorporated as part of British India, for the next 90 years until 1947.
Pike's Peak Gold Rush: Fifty-Niners stream into the Rocky Mountains of the western United States.
July Camillo Benso, Count of Cavour goads Austria into attacking Sardinia.
Jacques Offenbach's operetta Orpheus in the Underworld, featuring music associated with the can-can, is first performed in Paris.
British merchant Thomas Blake Glover begins business in Nagasaki, Japan.
District nursing begins in Liverpool, England, when philanthropist William Rathbone employs Mary Robinson to nurse the sick poor in their own homes.
Aletschhorn, the second summit of the Bernese Alps, is first ascended.
German chemist Albert Niemann makes a detailed analysis of the coca leaf, isolating and purifying the alkaloid, which he calls cocaine.
October 26 Garibaldi again defeats the Neapolitan forces, advancing on Gaeta, the last remaining Neapolitan strong-point.
Discovery of the chemical elements: Robert Bunsen discovers caesium and rubidium.
Trent Affair – The USS San Jacinto stops the United Kingdom mail ship Trent, and arrests two Confederate envoys, James Mason and John Slidell, sparking a diplomatic crisis between the U.K. and U.S.
First description of Archaeopteryx, based on a feather found in Bavaria; in September the first complete identified skeleton is found near Langenaltheim in Germany.
Abdülmecid I, Sultan of the Ottoman Empire (1839–____) dies and is succeeded by Abdülaziz (____–1876). June 25: Abdülaziz
American Civil War: June 1 – Battle of Fair Oaks – Both sides claim victory.
Richard Jordan Gatling patents the Gatling gun in the United States.
American Civil War: August 28–30 – Second Battle of Bull Run – Confederate forces inflict a crushing defeat on Union General John Pope.
The first Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe horse race is held.
Linoleum is patented in the United Kingdom.
The Colmar Treasure, a hoard of 14th century Jewish precious metal objects, is discovered in Alsace.
The Union suffers one of its costliest defeats at the Battle of Olustee near Lake City, Florida.
An annular solar eclipse occurs, the 42nd solar eclipse of Solar Saros 131.
Montana Territory is organized out of parts of Washington Territory and Dakota Territory.
American Civil War: Confederate States Army General Robert E. Lee surrenders to Union Army General Ulysses S. Grant at Appomattox Court House, effectively ending the war.
July 5 The U.S. Secret Service is founded.
Nottingham Forest Football Club, an association football based in West Bridgford, Nottingham, England, is founded.
The SS Great Eastern successfully completes laying the transatlantic telegraph cable between Valentia Island, Ireland, and Heart's Content, Newfoundland, permanently restoring a communications link.
Princess Helena, third daughter of Queen Victoria, marries Prince Christian of Schleswig-Holstein.
January 1 Fisk University, a historically black university, is established in Nashville, Tennessee.
A firing squad executes Emperor Maximilian I of Mexico and two of his lieutenants.
The three 'Manchester Martyrs' are hanged in England for the murder of a policeman whilst attempting to rescue two Irish Republican Brotherhood members from imprisonment on 18 September.
Benito Juárez becomes Mexican president again.
Maryland School for the Deaf is established.
Pedro Figueredo creates the Cuban national anthem, El Himno de Bayamo.
Time zone: New Zealand officially adopts a standard time, to be observed nationally.
The fortress of Goryōkaku is turned over to Imperial Japanese forces, bringing an end to the Republic of Ezo, the Battle of Hakodate and the Boshin War, the military phase of the Meiji Restoration.
Arabella Mansfield became the first woman in the United States awarded a license to practice law, at Mount Pleasant, Iowa.
The first game of American football between two American colleges is played. Rutgers University defeats Princeton University 6–4, in a forerunner to American football and College football.
English novelist Charles Dickens dies at Gads Hill Place in Kent, leaving his last book, The Mystery of Edwin Drood, unfinished.
Schoolteacher Seraph Young Ford becomes the first woman to vote under a women’s equal suffrage law in the USA, casting her ballot in the Salt Lake City municipal election.

A Chilean prospecting party led by José Díaz Gana discovers the silver ores of Caracoles in the Bolivian portion of Atacama Desert, leading to the last of Chilean silver rushes and a diplomatic dispute over its taxation between Chile and Bolivia.

c. November – The South Improvement Company is formed in Pennsylvania by John D. Rockefeller and a group of major United States railroad interests, in an early effort to organize and control the American petroleum industry.
March 21 Otto von Bismarck becomes the first Chancellor of the German Empire.
The first Rugby Union International results in a 1–0 win, by Scotland over England.
Shiseido Pharmacy Shop, predecessor of Shiseido, a major worldwide cosmetics brand, is founded in Ginza, Tokyo, Japan.
University College Wales (later to become Aberystwyth University) begins its first academic session.
The first Shriners Temple (called Mecca) is established in New York City.
The California Penal Code goes into effect.
February 20 The University of California opens its first medical school in San Francisco.
January 1 Japan adopts the Gregorian calendar.
John Ernst Worrell Keely demonstrates his "induction resonance motion motor", a perpetual motion machine, which eventually turns out to be a fraud.
The Supreme Council 33° Ancient and Accepted Scottish Rite of Freemasonry of Canada is founded.
Schindler Group, known for escalators and elevators, is founded in Switzerland.
'Albert's swarm' of Rocky Mountain locusts begins to devastate the western United States.
Aristides wins the first Kentucky Derby.
The Metre Convention is signed in Paris, France.
In Northfield, Minnesota, Jesse James and the James–Younger Gang attempt to rob the town's bank, but are surrounded by an angry mob and nearly wiped out.
Adolphus Busch's brewery, Anheuser-Busch in St. Louis, Missouri, first markets Budweiser, a pale lager, as a nationally sold beer.
Alexander Graham Bell is granted a United States patent for the telephone.
For the only time in history, The Boat Race between the Universities of Oxford and Cambridge is declared a "dead heat" (i.e., a draw).
Henrik Ibsen's first contemporary realist drama The Pillars of Society is premièred at the Odense Teater.
Brahms' Symphony No. 2 premieres in Vienna.
In Strasbourg, Alsace-Lorraine, the much studied stele of the Roman legionary Caius Largennius is discovered.
The Wallingford Tornado of ____, the deadliest tornado in Connecticut history, destroys the town of Wallingford, killing 34 people and injuring more than 70.
Gajanan Maharaj appears at Shegaon, Maharashtra.
The Stefan–Boltzmann law is discovered by Jozef Stefan.
Using a filament of carbonized thread, Thomas Edison tests his first practical electric light bulb (it lasts 13+1⁄2 hours before burning out).

University of Nebraska Cornhusker Marching Band is founded in Lincoln, Nebraska. The group will go on to perform the first football halftime show in 1892 and, under Director Donald A. Lentz, invent Band Day.
The Transvaal becomes a republic, and Paul Kruger becomes its first president.
The Gokstad ship is found in Norway, the first Viking ship burial to be excavated.
First Boer War: The Battle of Bronkhorstspruit results in a Boer victory over the British.
The fifth hurricane of the Atlantic season hits Florida and the Carolinas, killing about 700.

Determined to bring about the revival of the Hebrew language as a way of unifying Jews, Eliezer Ben-Yehuda has what is believed to be the first conversation in Modern Hebrew, with friends living in Paris.
The satirical magazine Judge is first published in the United States.
The British Chartered Institute of Patent Agents (the modern-day Chartered Institute of Patent Attorneys) is founded.
Charles J. Guiteau, the murderer of President James A. Garfield, is sentenced to death (found guilty on January 25), despite an insanity defense raised by his lawyer.
Zulu king Cetshwayo kaMpande returns to South Africa from England.
Whitman College is chartered as a 4-year college in Walla Walla, Washington.
Zulu King Cetshwayo barely escapes a rebel attack with his life.

In five Civil Rights Cases the Supreme Court of the United States declares part of the Civil Rights Act of 1875 to be unconstitutional, allowing individuals and corporations to discriminate based on race.
Police training schools are established in every prefecture in Japan.

The Siege of Khartoum begins in the Sudan as part of the Mahdist War: an Egyptian garrison led by British General Charles Gordon and Sudanese civilians are besieged by Mahdist forces; the city falls on January 26, 1885.
The first tennis tournaments, held in the grounds of Shrubland Hall, Leamington Spa, England, are recorded in today's Courier.
September 18 The union of Eastern Rumelia with Bulgaria is proclaimed at Plovdiv.
The Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Madrid is founded.
Mahdist War in Sudan: Battle of Abu Klea – British troops defeat Mahdist forces.
Karl Benz patents the first successful gasoline-driven automobile, the Benz Patent-Motorwagen (built in 1885).
Bedford Rugby Club is formed in England.
American Indian Wars: After almost 30 years of fighting, Apache leader Geronimo surrenders, with his last band of warriors, to General Nelson Miles, at Skeleton Canyon in Arizona.
The earliest constituent of the U.S. National Institutes of Health is established at the Marine Hospital, Staten Island, as the Laboratory of Hygiene.
The International Bureau of Intellectual Property is established.
Tokyo Fire Insurance, predecessor of Sompo Japan Nipponkoa Insurance, is founded.
The first Saint Verhaegen takes place in Brussels.
Abbott Laboratories as Abbott Alkaloidal by Dr. Wallace C. Abbott in Illinois.
In a letter accepting renomination as President of the United States, Grover Cleveland declares the Chinese "impossible of assimilation with our people and dangerous to our peace and welfare".
First Inter-Parliamentary Conference held.
Gustav Mahler premieres his Symphony No. 1, in Budapest.
British chemists Frederick Abel and James Dewar file their first patent for the smokeless propellant cordite.
The Uddevalla Suffrage Association is founded in Sweden, with a formal founding event on November 2 a month later.
Francis Galton announces a statistical demonstration of the uniqueness and classifiability of individual human fingerprints.
The first ever official English County Championship cricket match begins in Bristol; Yorkshire beats Gloucestershire, by eight wickets.
January 5 The Australian shearers' strike, that leads indirectly to the foundation of the Australian Labor Party, begins.
Germany takes formal possession of its new African territories.
Jim Hogg becomes the first native Texan to be governor of that state.
The 'Little Pastry Chef', a French police informant among anarchists, is murdered in Saint-Denis.
An oil fire in Oil City, Pennsylvania, United States, kills 130 people.
The Historical American Exposition opens in Madrid.
Brothers Charles and Frank Duryea drive the first gasoline-powered motorcar in America, on public roads in Springfield, Massachusetts.
Evergreen Park, Illinois, is incorporated.
June 20 The Wengernalpbahn railway in Wengen, Switzerland (Canton of Bern) is opened.
Oil is discovered on the Osage Indian reservation, making the Osage the "richest group of people in the world".
Richard Strauss marries Pauline De Ahna.

Ère des attentats: Café Terminus attack – In one of the first acts of modern terrorism, French anarchist Émile Henry throws a bomb into the Café Terminus in Paris, France, killing one person and injuring at least seventeen.
The Aljaž Tower, a symbol of the Slovenes, is erected on Mount Triglav.
At the Swedish-Norwegian Club in Paris, Alfred Nobel signs his last will and testament, setting aside his estate to establish the Nobel Prize after his death.
Flamengo, a well known professional football club in Brazil, is officially founded.
Responding to national outrage at the defeat at Adwa, Italian Prime Minister Francesco Crispi resigns.

May 26 Coronation of Nicholas II and Alexandra Feodorovna in the Dormition Cathedral, Moscow; this will be the last coronation of the Russian monarch. The Qing dynasty is represented at the coronation by Chinese official Li Hongzhang who through September goes on to visit Germany, Britain, Canada and the United States.
The International Socialist Workers and Trade Union Congress opens in London.
The play Cyrano de Bergerac, by Edmond Rostand, premieres in Paris.
The Klondike Gold Rush begins when the first successful prospectors arrive in Seattle

A patent is awarded for the invention of the first automotive muffler, with the granting by the U.S. Patent Office of application number 582,485 to Milton Reeves and his brother Marshall T. Reeves, of the Reeves Pulley Company of Columbus, Indiana.
The Academia Militar, precursor to the Philippine Military Academy, is founded at Malolos by decree of Philippine Republic President Emilio Aguinaldo.
The Spanish peace commissioners in Paris announce that they accept the offer of the U.S. to purchase the Philippines.
The Lutheran Church of the Redeemer, Jerusalem, is dedicated after the Sultan of the Ottoman Empire presents the area, said to be the site of the Virgin Mary's home, to Germany's Roman Catholics.
Cyclone Mahina strikes Bathurst Bay, Queensland. A 12-meter-high wave reaches up to 5 km inland, leaving over 400 dead (one of the deadliest natural disasters in Australia's history).
Lord Kitchener is appointed as the British Governor of the Anglo-Egyptian Sudan.
Britain formally claims possession of the "New Territories" as an extension of its lease of Hong Kong to cover the area south of the Sham Chun River and 230 islands in Kowloon Bay.
Second Boer War: Battle of Spion Kop – Boer troops defeat the British Army.
The Federation of Australia is enacted, marking the unification of its colonies into a single country.
FC Bayern, Germany's most successful football club, is founded in Munich.
The Intercollegiate Prohibition Association is established in Chicago.
The ____ Census of India is taken, the fourth, and first reliable, census of the British Raj.
William McKinley assassination: American anarchist Leon Czolgosz shoots U.S. President William McKinley at the Pan-American Exposition in Buffalo, New York. McKinley dies 8 days later.
Hakkōda Mountains incident: A snowstorm in the Hakkōda Mountains of northern Honshu, Japan, kills 199 during a military training exercise.
Australian officers Breaker Morant and Peter Handcock are executed for the murder of Boer prisoners of war near Louis Trichardt.
De'Longhi home appliance brand is founded in Treviso, Italy.
American motorcycle brand Harley-Davidson is founded in Wisconsin.
Atlético Madrid is founded as a professional association football club in Spain.
The town of Heppner, Oregon is nearly destroyed by a cloud burst that results in a flash flood that kills about 238 people.
In Germany: The Kaiser-Friedrich-Museum opens in Berlin for the display of fine art.
Phi Delta Epsilon, the international medical fraternity, is founded by Aaron Brown and 8 of his friends, at Cornell University Medical College.
April 8 The Entente Cordiale is signed between the UK and France.

"The greatest ball of the Gilded Age" is held by James Hazen Hyde, the heir to the fortune of the founder of the Equitable Life Assurance Association, at New York City's Sherry Hotel, spending $200,000 for a "Louis XV costume ball."
The Institute of Musical Art, predecessor of the Juilliard School, opens in New York City.
The sinking of the Japanese ferry Kinjo Maru kills 160 people after the British ship HMS Baralong collides with it in the Sea of Japan.
Georgian Socialist-Federalist Revolutionary Party under the leadership of Leo Kereselidze successfully robs the Dusheti treasury of the Russian Empire, one of the largest expropriations of the time.
Mahatma Gandhi coins the term Satyagraha, to characterize the nonviolence movement in South Africa.
In the Russian Empire, the Fundamental Laws are announced at the first state Duma.
c. March 28 – The volcano Ksudach erupts, in the Kamchatka Peninsula.
The Prime Minister of Bulgaria, Dimitar Petkov, is assassinated by an anarchist in Sofia.
Pope Pius X promulgates the encyclical Pascendi Dominici gregis, opposing modernism in the Catholic Church.
SOS comes into force internationally as a distress signal (originally for ship-to-shore wireless telegraphy).
Construction begins on the RMS Olympic, at the Harland and Wolff Shipyard in Belfast.
The Franco-British Exhibition (____) is held in London.
Adana massacre: Ottoman Turks kill 15,000–30,000 Armenian Christians, in the Adana Vilayet.
Einar Dessau uses a shortwave radio transmitter in Denmark.
Karl Landsteiner, Constantin Levaditi and Erwin Popper first isolate the poliovirus.
Great January Comet of ____ first observed (perihelion: January 17).
Hitachi, an electromechanics company, is founded as a mining machine repair factory in Ibaraki Prefecture, Japan.
Main article: December ____ December 3 – Modern neon lighting is first demonstrated by Georges Claude at the Paris Motor Show.
The Australasian Antarctic Expedition begins as the SY Aurora departs London.
The Delhi Durbar is held to mark the coronation of George V and Queen Mary as Emperor and Empress of India, and the transfer of the capital of British India from Calcutta to Delhi.

Main article: January ____ January 3: Siege of Sidney Street in London January 1 – A decade after federation, the Northern Territory and the Australian Capital Territory are added to the Commonwealth of Australia.
January 18 (Old Style January 5) – Prague Conference: Vladimir Lenin and the Bolshevik Party break away from the rest of the Russian Social Democratic Labour Party.
Regina Cyclone: Canada's deadliest tornado strikes Regina, Saskatchewan, killing 28 people.
The First International Opium Convention is signed at The Hague to restrict exports.
September 9 In Germany, BASF starts the world's first plant for the production of fertilizer based on the Haber-Bosch process, feeding in modern times about a third of the world's population.

Death Valley, California hits 134 °F (~56.7 °C), the all-time highest temperature recorded on Earth (although its validity has been challenged, and in 2020 a temperature of 54.4 °C (129.9 °F) was recorded at the same location, which would make it the world's highest verified air temperature, subject to confirmation).
Mexican Revolution: Pancho Villa returns to Mexico from his self-imposed exile in the United States.
The Karluk, flagship of the Canadian Arctic Expedition, sinks after being crushed by ice.
Felix Ysagun Manalo registers the Iglesia ni Cristo (Church of Christ) with the government of the Philippines.

The guns of Point Nepean fort at Port Phillip Heads in Victoria (Australia) fire across the bows of the Norddeutscher Lloyd steamer SS Pfalz, which is attempting to leave the Port of Melbourne in ignorance of the declaration of war, and she is detained; this is said to be the first Allied shot of the war.
Armenian genocide: 17,000 civilians from the Ottoman Armenian population of Trebizond are massacred by Ottoman Turks.
WWI: Caucasus Campaign – Battle of Sarikamish: Russia defeats Ottoman Turkey.
WWI: Start of Second Battle of Ypres – Germany makes its first large scale use of poison gas on the Western Front.
WWI: Paris is bombed by German zeppelins.
Main article: January ____ January 1 – The British Royal Army Medical Corps carries out the first successful blood transfusion, using blood that has been stored and cooled.
The Romanian Association football club Sportul Studențesc is founded in Bucharest.
A tornado strikes Mattoon, Illinois, causing devastation and killing 101 people.
The enactment of the Jones Act grants Puerto Ricans United States citizenship.
WWI: Canadian troops win the Battle of Vimy Ridge.
Russia switches from the Julian calendar to the Gregorian calendar; the date skips from January 31 to February 14.
Main article: November ____ November 1 The Polish–Ukrainian War is inaugurated, by the proclamation of the West Ukrainian People's Republic in Galicia, with a capital at Lwów.
WWI: Gavrilo Princip, assassin of Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria, dies in Terezín, Austria-Hungary, after three years in prison.
Winnipeg general strike: Workers in Winnipeg, Canada, begin a strike for better wages and working conditions; the strike lasts for six weeks.
January 19 The Monarchy of the North is established in Northern Portugal.
Epsom Riot by Canadian troops: English Police Sergeant Thomas Green is killed.

Main article: October ____ October – English writer Agatha Christie's first novel, The Mysterious Affair at Styles, appears in the U.S., introducing her long-running Belgian detective character Hercule Poirot in the setting of an English country house. (The book is published in the U.K. in 1921.)
Agnès Souret is elected "The most beautiful woman in France", retrospectively considered the first Miss France.
Lithuanian Peace Treaty: The Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic recognizes independent Lithuania.
Researchers at the University of Toronto, led by biochemist Frederick Banting, announce the discovery of the hormone insulin.
R38-class airship ZR-2 explodes on her fourth test flight near Kingston upon Hull, England, killing 44 of the 49 Anglo-American crew on board.
The Spanish liner Santa Isabel breaks in two and sinks off Villa Garcia, Mexico, with the loss of 244 of the 300 people on board.
Italian forces occupy Misrata, Libya; the reconquest of Libya begins.
Baragoola, the last of the Binngarra class Manly ferries, is launched at Balmain, New South Wales.

____ Picardie mid-air collision: The first midair collision between airliners occurs, between a Daimler Airway de Havilland DH.18 and a Grands Express Aériens Farman Goliath over Poix-de-Picardie, Amiens, France.
October 13 Ankara replaces Istanbul (Constantinople), as the capital of Turkey.
Newspaper printers strike in New York City.
Vice President Calvin Coolidge becomes the 30th president of the United States, upon the death of President Warren G. Harding in San Francisco.
United States occupation of the Dominican Republic (1916–24) comes to an end. The constitutional government headed by General Horacio Vázquez, elected in the elections held in March, is established.
Main article: October ____ October – The skull of the Taung Child is discovered.
The Computing-Tabulating-Recording Company (CTR), based in the U.S. state of New York, is renamed International Business Machines (IBM).
The Patent and Trademark Office is transferred to the Department of Commerce.
Fritz Haarmann, a serial killer convicted of the murder of 24 boys and young men, is guillotined in Germany.
Pink's War: The British Royal Air Force bombards mountain strongholds of Mahsud tribesmen in South Waziristan.
December 26 In the history of Japan, the Shōwa period begins from this day, due to the death of Emperor Taishō on the day before. His son Hirohito will reign as Emperor of Japan until 1989.

Nicaraguan President Adolfo Díaz requests U.S. military assistance in the ongoing civil war. American peacekeeping troops immediately set up neutral zones in Puerto Cabezas and at the mouth of the Rio Grande to protect American and foreign lives and property.
A Caudron C.61 aircraft, operated by Compagnie Internationale de Navigation Aérienne, crashes in Czechoslovakia.
Australian cricketer Bill Ponsford makes 437 runs to break his own world record for the highest first-class cricket score at Melbourne Cricket Ground.
The first Asian commuter metro line, the Tokyo Metro Ginza Line, opens in Japan.
Yugoslavia severs diplomatic relations with Albania.
The first east–west transatlantic flight by aeroplane takes place from Dublin, Ireland, to Greenly Island, Canada, using the German Junkers W 33 Bremen.

The first regular schedule of television programming begins in Schenectady, New York, by General Electric's television station W2XB (the station is popularly known as WGY Television, after its sister radio station WGY).
Arvid Lindman returns as Prime Minister of Sweden, with his right-wing rival Ernst Trygger as Foreign Minister of Sweden.
The government of Aristide Briand falls in France.

Main article: August ____ August 8–29 – German rigid airship LZ 127 Graf Zeppelin makes a circumnavigation of the Northern Hemisphere eastabout out of Lakehurst, New Jersey, including the first nonstop flight of any kind across the Pacific Ocean (Tokyo–Los Angeles).
August 29 The ____ Palestine riots: 18–20 Jews are killed in Safed by Palestinian Arabs.
Main article: July ____ July 3–10 – The First Eastern Women's Congress takes place in Damascus in Syria.
The Kurdish Ararat rebellion is suppressed by the Turks.
Amy Johnson lands in Darwin, Australia, becoming the first woman to fly solo from England to Australia (she left on May 5 for the 11,000 mile flight).
Main article: December ____ December 5 – The original Cathedral of Christ the Saviour in Moscow (1883) is demolished, by order of Joseph Stalin.
German pilot Elly Beinhorn begins her flight to Africa.
Main article: May ____ May 1: Empire State Building is completed. May 1 – Construction of the Empire State Building is completed in New York City.
Hermann Göring is elected as Speaker of the German Reichstag.
February 4 The ____ Winter Olympics open in Lake Placid, New York.
The Emu War, a nuisance wildlife management military operation, begins in Australia.
American airship Akron crashes off the coast of New Jersey, killing 73 of its 76 crewmen. It is the worst aviation accident in history up to this date (and until 1950).
Japan announces it will leave the League of Nations (due to a cancellation period of exactly two years, the egression becomes effective March 27, 1935).
Aviator Bill Lancaster takes off from Lympne in England, in an attempt to make a speed record to the Cape of Good Hope, but vanishes (his body is not found in the Sahara Desert until 1962).
Kimon Georgiev stages a coup d'état in Bulgaria.
King Leopold III of Belgium succeeds to the throne, following the death (February 17) of his father King Albert I.
Daniel Salamanca Urey, President of Bolivia, is deposed in a military coup, and replaced by José Luis Tejada Sorzano.
Sun Myung Moon, a teenage Presbyterian convert in Korea under Japanese rule, claims to have a revelation from Jesus, telling him to complete his mission from almost 2,000 years ago.
December 12 The Lebensborn program in support of Nazi eugenics is founded by Heinrich Himmler in Germany.
The first canned beer is sold in Richmond, Virginia, United States, by Gottfried Krueger Brewing Company.

The Montreux Convention Regarding the Regime of the Straits is signed in Montreux, allowing Turkey to fortify the Dardanelles and the Bosphorus, but guaranteeing free passage to ships of all nations in peacetime.

September 9 ____ Naval Revolt (Portugal): The crews of Portuguese Navy frigate NRP Afonso de Albuquerque and destroyer Dão mutiny while anchored in Lisbon Harbour. Opposed to the Salazar dictatorship's support of General Franco's coup in Spain, they declare their solidarity with the Second Spanish Republic.

The first World Speedway Championship is held at Wembley Stadium in London, England. It is won by Australian Lionel Van Praag, with Englishman Eric Langton second and Australian Bluey Wilkinson third.
The Dandy comic is first published in Scotland; it continued until 2012 as a physical publication, then online until 2013.

Spanish Civil War: The Bombing of Guernica is carried out in Spain, by the Condor Legion of the Nazi German Luftwaffe, in support of the Francoists. Three-quarters of the town is destroyed and hundreds killed.
Walt Disney's Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs, the world's first feature-length cel animated film, premieres at the Carthay Circle Theatre in Los Angeles.
Mussolini makes a speech in Trieste, Italy, where he indicates that Italy is supporting Germany in the Sudeten crisis.
October 5 Edvard Beneš, president of Czechoslovakia, resigns.

German troops march into the Sudetenland. The Polish government gives the Czech government an ultimatum, stating that Trans-Olza region must be handed over within twenty-four hours. The Czechs have little choice but to comply; Polish forces occupy Trans-Olza.
The first issue of Fashizmi is published in Tirana, Albania.
Main article: July ____ July 4 – The Neuengamme concentration camp becomes autonomous.
WWII: South Africa declares war on Germany.
In Tacoma, Washington, the 600-foot (180 m)-long center span of the Tacoma Narrows Bridge (known as Galloping Gertie) collapses.

Jilava Massacre: In Romania, coup leader General Ion Antonescu's Iron Guard arrests and executes over 60 of exiled King Carol II of Romania's aides, starting at a penitentiary near Bucharest. Among the dead is former minister and acclaimed historian Nicolae Iorga.
King Haakon VII of Norway and his government are evacuated from Tromsø to London, on HMS Devonshire.
Sweden's low temperature record of −53 °C is set in a village within the Vilhelmina Municipality.
WWII: Italian aircraft damage the British destroyer HMS Fearless which has to be sunk in the Mediterranean.
WWII: Battle of Keren – British and Free French Forces fight hard to capture the strategic town of Keren in Italian Eritrea.
WWII: The Japanese establish a beachhead on the north coast of New Guinea in the Buna-Gona area; a small Australian force begins a rearguard action on the Kokoda Track campaign.

WWII: Battle of Slim River: Japanese forces of the 5th Division, supported by tanks, sweep through sixteen miles of British defenses, shattering the exhausted 11th Indian Division and inflicting some 3,000 casualties.
WWII: Second Battle of El Alamein: British troops go on the offensive against the Axis forces.

WWII: Bombing of Kassel in World War II: The British Royal Air Force delivers a highly destructive airstrike on the German industrial and population center of Kassel; at least 10,000 are killed and 150,000 are made homeless.
The Holocaust: Uprising in Sobibór extermination camp; about half the inmates escape. Three days later, the camp is closed.
American critic and commentator Alexander Woollcott suffers an eventually fatal heart attack, during a regular broadcast of the CBS Radio round-table program People's Platform.
WWII: British light cruiser HMS Penelope is torpedoed and sunk by U-410 in the Mediterranean; 417 of her crew, including the captain, go down with the ship; 206 survive.
On the middle front of the Gothic Line, Brazilian troops control the Serchio valley region after 10 days of fighting.
Port Chicago disaster: The SS E. A. Bryan, loaded with ammunition, explodes at the Port Chicago, California, Naval Magazine, killing 320 sailors and civilian personnel.
The Franck Committee recommends against a surprise nuclear bombing of Japan.
Troops of the Yugoslav 4th Army, together with the Slovene 9th Corpus NOV, enter Trieste.
Inundation of the Wieringermeer in the Netherlands by occupying German forces.
The Emirate of Transjordan becomes the Hashemite Kingdom of Transjordan when its parliament makes the ruling amir Abdullah their king on the day it ratifies the Treaty of London.
The Bell 47 becomes the first helicopter certified for civilian use in the United States
The first artificial, self-sustaining nuclear chain reaction in Europe is initiated, within the Soviet (Russian) nuclear reactor F-1.
King Michael I of Romania is forced to abdicate and the Romanian monarchy is abolished.

In Paris, France, peace treaties are signed between the World War II Allies and Italy, Hungary, Romania, Bulgaria and Finland. Italy cedes most of Istria to the Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia (later Croatia).
Muhammad Ali Jinnah becomes the first governor-general of Pakistan. Liaquat Ali Khan takes office as the first prime minister of Pakistan.
Harry Dexter White and Donald Hiss refute allegations of Communism by Whittaker Chambers, before the HUAC.
Main article: May ____ May – The RAND Corporation is established, as an independent nonprofit policy research and analysis institution, in the United States.
Main article: July ____ July 5 – The National Health Service in the United Kingdom begins functioning, giving the right to universal healthcare, free at point of use.
The ____ Queen Charlotte Islands earthquake is Canada's largest earthquake since the 1700 Cascadia earthquake.

The Foley Square trial of Eugene Dennis and ten other leaders of the Communist Party USA ends in New York City (the longest trial in U.S. history to this date); all defendants are found guilty and all but one sentenced to five years of prison.
Forces from the Chinese Communist Party enter Beijing.
Hangang Bridge bombing: The South Korean army, in an attempt to defend Seoul, blows up the Hangang Bridge while it is crowded with refugees.
May 22 Celâl Bayar becomes the third president of Turkey and Adnan Menderes of the DP forms the new government of Turkey (19th government).
In India, the first Cabinet Secretary (N. R. Pillai) is appointed.
March 29 Second Red Scare: In the United States, Julius and Ethel Rosenberg are convicted of conspiracy to commit espionage. On April 5 they are sentenced to death.
The Organization of Central American States (Organización de Estados Centroamericanos, ODECA) is formed.
The Marshall Plan expires, after distributing more than $13.3 billion US in foreign aid to rebuild Europe.
The crash of a United States Air Force C-124 Globemaster at Moses Lake, WA kills 86 servicemen.
The Special Forces (United States Army) are created.
The Pan-Malayan Labour Party is founded in Malaya, as a union of statewide labour parties.
Authorities at the Natural History Museum, London announce that the skull of Piltdown Man (allegedly an early human discovered in 1912) is a hoax.
Aldous Huxley first tries the psychedelic hallucinogen mescaline, inspiring his book The Doors of Perception.
The first German prisoners of war return from the Soviet Union to West Germany.
U.S. President Dwight D. Eisenhower dedicates the USMC War Memorial (Iwo Jima Memorial), at the Arlington National Cemetery.
"Miracle of Bern": West Germany beats Hungary 3–2 to win the ____ FIFA World Cup.
The main immigration port-of-entry in New York Harbor at Ellis Island closes permanently.
December 14 The Tappan Zee Bridge over the Hudson River, in New York State, opens to traffic.
The first franchised McDonald's restaurant is opened by Ray Kroc, in Des Plaines, Illinois.

Taekwondo, a form of Korean martial arts, is officially recognized in South Korea. A board convened by General Choi Hong Hi, including master instructors, historians and societal leaders, officially adopts the name Taekwon-Do for the unified Korean martial art.
Browder v. Gayle: The United States Supreme Court declares illegal the state and municipal laws requiring segregated buses in Montgomery, Alabama, thus ending the Montgomery bus boycott.
June 28 Poznań ____ protests: Labour riots in Poznań, Poland, are crushed with heavy loss of life. Soviet troops fire at a crowd protesting high prices, killing 53 people.
October 31 Suez Crisis: The United Kingdom and France begin bombing Egypt to force the reopening of the Suez Canal.
Asghar Khan becomes the first native Commander-in-Chief of the Pakistan Air Force and the world's youngest Air Vice Marshal at 36 years old.
The Treaty of Rome (Patto di Roma) establishes the European Economic Community (EEC; predecessor of the European Union) between Italy, France, West Germany, Belgium, the Netherlands and Luxembourg.
The sailing ship Pamir sinks off the Azores in a hurricane.

The British Overseas Airways Corporation (BOAC) uses one of its new de Havilland Comet 4s, G-APDB, to make the first commercial transatlantic flight by a jet airliner, from London to New York International Airport, Anderson Field via Gander.
The United Kingdom transfers sovereignty of Christmas Island from Singapore to Australia.
The Nordic Passport Union comes into force.
The Workers' Stadium sports venue in Beijing (China) is officially opened.
Charles Robberts Swart is appointed the 11th Governor-General of the Union of South Africa.
Main article: August ____ August 4 – Martial law is declared in Laos.
The Soviet Union national football team defeats the Yugoslavia national football team 2–1 in Paris to win the first UEFA European Championship.
Congolese President Joseph Kasa-Vubu dismisses Patrice Lumumba's entire government, and also places Lumumba under house arrest.
Middle Congo becomes independent from France, as the Republic of Congo (Congo-Brazzaville).
Ireland's first national television station, Telefís Éireann (later Raidió Teilifís Éireann), begins broadcasting.
Supercar, the first family sci-fi TV series filmed in Supermarionation, debuts on ATV in the UK.
South African golfer Gary Player becomes the first non-American to win the Masters Tournament.

The Commonwealth Immigrants Act in the United Kingdom removes free immigration from the citizens of member states of the Commonwealth of Nations, requiring proof of employment in the U.K. This comes into effect on 1 July.
Heavy storms and high tides result in the North Sea flood of ____ on Germany's North Sea coast, mainly around Hamburg; more than 300 people die and thousands lose their homes.
The last soldiers of the French Foreign Legion leave Algeria.
Ivan Sutherland writes the revolutionary Sketchpad program and runs it on the Lincoln TX-2 computer at Massachusetts Institute of Technology.
The first annual National Hockey League Entry Draft is held in Montreal, Canada.
American test pilot Joe Walker again achieves a sub-orbital spaceflight according to international standards, this time by piloting the X-15 to an altitude of 67.0 miles (107.8 kilometers).
The Charter of the Palestine Liberation Organization (PLO) is released by the Arab League.
South Korean President Park Chung Hee reshuffles his Cabinet, after a series of student demonstrations against his efforts to restore diplomatic and trade relations with Japan.
April 20 U.S. President Lyndon B. Johnson in New York, and Soviet Premier Nikita Khrushchev in Moscow, simultaneously announce plans to cut back production of materials for making nuclear weapons.
President Kenneth Kaunda of Zambia announces that Zambia and the United Kingdom have agreed on a deadline before which the Rhodesian white government should be ousted.

Pakistani Forces achieve a decisive victory at the Battle of Chawinda, ultimately halting the Indian advance and successfully stabilizing the Sialkot Front; it is the world's largest tank battle since the Battle of Kursk in the Second World War between Nazi Germany and the Soviet Union.
The British governor of Aden cancels the constitution and takes direct control of the protectorate, due to the bad security situation.
Lise Meitner and Otto Hahn are awarded the Fermi Prize.
Spain declares a general amnesty for crimes committed during the Spanish Civil War, effective only for the Falangists' side.
The military government of Nigeria announces that ex-prime minister Abubakar Tafawa Balewa was killed during the coup a week previously.
Ilse Koch, known as the "Witch of Buchenwald", commits suicide in the Bavarian prison of Aichach.

25 May – Celtic F.C. defeat Inter Milan 2–1 in Lisbon to win the European Cup, becoming the first British football club to win the competition. The team, later nicknamed the Lisbon Lions, was composed entirely of players born within 30 miles of Glasgow.
Louis Leakey announces the discovery of pre-human fossils in Kenya; he names the species Kenyapithecus africanus.

April 4 Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.: Martin Luther King Jr. is shot dead at the Lorraine Motel in Memphis, Tennessee by James Earl Ray. King-assassination riots erupt in major American cities, lasting for several days afterwards.
Vietnam War: Phong Nhị and Phong Nhất massacre.
The Vienna Convention on Road Signs and Signals is signed and ratified.
Doo, Where Are You! premieres on CBS, starting the Scooby-Doo franchise.
The Montreal Expos become Major League Baseball's first team outside the United States.

The body of former United States General and President Dwight D. Eisenhower is brought by caisson to the United States Capitol to lie in state in the Capitol Rotunda; Eisenhower had died two days earlier, after a long illness, in the Walter Reed Army Medical Center, Washington, D.C.
Feyenoord win the European Cup in association football after a 2–1 win over Celtic.
President of India V. V. Giri declares new elections.
July 3 All 112 people on board Dan-Air Flight 1903 are killed when the chartered British De Havilland Comet crashes into mountains north of Barcelona through navigational error.
A coup attempt is exposed and foiled in Egypt.
Operation Lam Son 719: South Vietnamese General Do Cao Tri is killed in a helicopter crash en route to taking control of the faltering campaign.
The McDonnell Douglas DC-10 enters service with American Airlines.

December 23 The 6.2 Mw Nicaragua earthquake kills 5,000–11,000 people in the capital Managua. President Anastasio Somoza Debayle is later accused of not distributing millions of dollars worth of foreign aid.
Vietnam War: Nguyen Hue Offensive – Prompted by the North Vietnamese offensive, the United States resumes bombing of Hanoi and Haiphong.
The United Nations Conference on the Human Environment is held in Stockholm, Sweden
Naim Talu, a former civil servant, forms the new government of Turkey (36th government).
A very long total solar eclipse occurs. During the entire second millennium, only seven total solar eclipses exceeded seven minutes of totality.
A British government White Paper on Northern Ireland proposes the re-establishment of an Assembly elected by proportional representation, with a possible All-Ireland council.
Main article: January ____ Main article: February ____ January 25 – Bülent Ecevit of CHP forms the new government of Turkey (37th government, partner MSP).
The Kiryat Shmona massacre takes place in Israel.

Birmingham pub bombings: In Birmingham, England, two pubs are bombed, killing 21 people in an attack widely believed at the time to be linked to the Provisional Irish Republican Army. The Birmingham Six are later sentenced to life in prison for this, but their convictions are quashed after a lengthy campaign.
Cape Verde, Mozambique and São Tomé and Príncipe join the United Nations.
In Brazil, Guanabara State merges into the state of Rio de Janeiro. The state's capital moves from the city of Niterói to the city of Rio de Janeiro.
A bomb blast at Samastipur, Bihar, India, fatally wounds Lalit Narayan Mishra, Minister of Railways.
The Pol Pot regime proclaims a new constitution for Democratic Kampuchea.
The Japanese general election takes place, and the ruling Liberal Democratic Party loses its majority in the 511-member House of Representatives, but remains the largest party with 249 seats.

Bradford Bishop allegedly murders five of his family members in Bethesda, Maryland. The crime goes undiscovered for 10 days and the suspect is never caught. From 2014 to 2018 he is on the FBI Ten Most Wanted Fugitives list.
The Pinochet dictatorship in Chile organises the youth event of Acto de Chacarillas, a ritualised act reminiscent of Francoist Spain.
London Transport's Silver Jubilee AEC Routemaster buses are launched.

August 15 The Big Ear, a radio telescope operated by Ohio State University as part of the SETI project, receives a radio signal from deep space; the event is named the Wow! signal for a notation made by a volunteer on the project.
Somalia mobilizes its troops to deal with an apparent Ethiopian attack.
In Acapulco, Mexico, Margaret Gardiner of South Africa is crowned Miss Universe.
Ali Soilih is found dead in the Comoros, allegedly shot when trying to escape.
The Guardian Angels are formed in New York City as an unarmed organization of young crime fighters.
Bauhaus releases their debut single "Bela Lugosi's Dead", considered to be the first gothic rock release.
Voyager 1 makes its closest approach to Jupiter at 277,000 kilometres (172,000 mi).
Global Positioning System time epoch begins at 00:00 UTC.
Proceedings on the Hague Convention on the Civil Aspects of International Child Abduction conclude at The Hague.
Paul Geidel, convicted of second-degree murder in 1911, is released from prison in Beacon, New York, after 68 years and 245 days (the longest-ever time served by an inmate).

Alfredo Rampi, a 6-year-old boy, falls into an artesian well in Vermicino, near Rome. After nearly three days of failed rescue attempts followed with bated breath from all over Italy, Alfredo dies inside the well, at a depth of 60 meters (200 ft).

The Space Shuttle program: Space Shuttle Columbia, with NASA astronauts John Young and Robert Crippen, launches on the STS-1 mission, returning to Earth on April 14. It is the first time a crewed reusable spacecraft has returned from orbit.

Pope John Paul II assassination attempt: Pope John Paul II is shot by Mehmet Ali Ağca, a Turkish gunman, as he enters St. Peter's Square in Vatican City to address a general audience. The Pope recovers.
Charles Haughey becomes Taoiseach of Ireland.
____ Thunderbirds Indian Springs Diamond Crash: Four Northrop T-38 aircraft of the United States Air Force crash at Indian Springs Air Force Auxiliary Field, Nevada, killing all 4 pilots.

British Airways Flight 9 suffers a temporary four-engine flameout and damage to the exterior of the plane after flying through the otherwise undetected volcanic ash plume from Indonesia's Mount Galunggung.
October 25 Invasion of Grenada by United States troops at the behest of Eugenia Charles of Dominica, a member of the Organization of American States.

100 women protest in Lahore, Pakistan, against military dictator Zia-ul-Haq's proposed Law of Evidence. The women are tear-gassed, baton-charged and thrown into lock-up but are successful in repealing the law.

Word processor software Multi-Tool Word, soon to become Microsoft Word, is released in the United States. It is primarily the work of programmers Richard Brodie and Charles Simonyi. Free demonstration copies on disk are distributed with the November issue of PC World magazine.
Argentinian footballer Diego Maradona is sold by FC Barcelona (Spain) to S.S.C. Napoli (Italy) for a world record fee at this date of $10.48M (£6.9M).
Joe Kittinger begins his attempt to become the first person to fly a gas balloon solo across the Atlantic Ocean.

Bhopal disaster: A methyl isocyanate leak from a Union Carbide pesticide plant in Bhopal, Madhya Pradesh, India, kills more than 8,000 people outright and injures over half a million (with more later dying from their injuries the death toll reaches 23,000+) in the worst industrial disaster in history.

June 14 TWA Flight 847, carrying 153 passengers from Athens to Rome, is hijacked by a Hezbollah fringe group. One passenger, U.S. Navy Petty Officer Robert Stethem, is killed. Greek police arrest a 65-year-old Lebanese suspect on September 21, 2019.
Approximately 10,000 people are killed when Bangladesh is affected by the storm surge from Tropical Storm One (1B).
Young driver Danny Sullivan beats veteran Mario Andretti to win the ____ Indianapolis 500.
The Rogers Commission releases its report on the Space Shuttle Challenger disaster.

People Power Revolution: President Ferdinand Marcos of the Philippines is ousted from power and goes into exile in Hawaii after 20 years of dictatorial rule; Corazon Aquino becomes the first Filipino woman president and forms an interim government with Salvador Laurel becoming her Vice-president and Prime Minister.
The Marshall Islands became an associated state under the Compact of Free Association.
The Parlatino Treaty of Institutionalization is signed.
Klaus Barbie goes on trial in Lyon for war crimes committed during World War II.
Martial law in Taiwan ends after 38 years.
The Birchandra Manu massacre occurs in Tripura, India.
Operation Flavius: A Special Air Service team of the British Army shoots dead 3 unarmed members of a Provisional Irish Republican Army (IRA) Active service unit in Gibraltar.
October 12 Walsh Street police shootings: Two Victoria Police officers are gunned down, execution style, in Australia.
December 3 The entire leadership of the ruling Socialist Unity Party in East Germany, including Egon Krenz, resigns. Hans Modrow becomes de facto the country's last leader.

April 19 Central Park jogger case: Trisha Meili is seriously assaulted and raped whilst jogging in New York City's Central Park; the convictions of five teenagers for the crime are vacated in 2002 (the jogger's identity remains secret for years, hence she is referred to as the "Central Park Jogger").
Main article: June ____ June 1–10 – Pope John Paul II visits Norway, Iceland, Finland, Denmark and Sweden.
